## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**❓ "Two requests for the same client hit two different servers at the exact same instant and it's the last token — who wins?"**
**The mechanism:** both servers call the same Lua script on the same Redis key. Redis is single-threaded per key, so it runs the two scripts one after another, not concurrently. The first decrements the last token and returns ALLOW; the second sees zero tokens and returns DENY.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "They line up at one counter that can only serve one at a time, so exactly one gets the last ticket."
**💬 One-liner:** "Redis serializes the atomic script on the key — exactly one wins, by construction."

**❓ "What happens when Redis goes down entirely?"**
**The mechanism:** each shard fails over to its replica in under a second. If the whole cluster is unreachable, gateways trip a circuit breaker and fall back to a per-node in-process token bucket sized at global_limit/num_nodes, emitting a fail_open alarm; fail-closed routes return 429 instead.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "We switch to a backup copy; if even that's gone, each server enforces a rough limit on its own and keeps the API up, while paging us."
**💬 One-liner:** "Failover to replica, then degrade to a local approximate limit — the limiter never takes the API down."

**❓ "How do you handle a flash crowd / one client suddenly doing 200K requests a second?"**
**The mechanism:** that's a hot key exceeding one shard's ~75K/s ceiling. Gateways lease blocks of tokens locally (cutting central ops ~100×), and for extreme cases we split the client's bucket across multiple shards, each metering a fraction of the limit (200K ÷ 10 = 20K/shard, under ceiling).
**🗣️ In plain words:** "We let each server grab a batch of allowances at once instead of asking the central counter every time, and for a giant client we spread its counter across several machines."
**💬 One-liner:** "Local token leasing plus key-splitting — no single shard ever becomes the bottleneck."

**❓ "Can the system over-count or under-count? What's your accuracy guarantee?"**
**The mechanism:** the atomic token bucket is exact per key under normal operation; bounded over-admission comes only from local leasing (≤ block × gateways on simultaneous crash) and per-region division. We state a ~±1% tolerance.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Normally it's exact; the only slack comes from the speed optimizations, and it's small and bounded."
**💬 One-liner:** "Exact at the core; the optimizations add a bounded ~1%, which I state up front."

**❓ "Can a client place the same request twice and be double-charged a token, or replay to get more?"**
**The mechanism:** the check itself is idempotent in effect only at the bucket level — each call spends a token. For request-level dedup (if needed), the gateway honors an idempotency key so a retried identical request isn't counted twice. The token spend is atomic, so no partial double-spend.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "A genuine retry can carry an idempotency key so we don't count it twice; the token math itself can't half-happen."
**💬 One-liner:** "Atomic spend plus optional idempotency key on retries — no double counting."

**❓ "How do you stop someone forging a client_id to get a bigger limit?"**
**The mechanism:** identity is never taken from the request body; it's derived from a verified API key or signed JWT at the gateway. The limit is looked up server-side from that verified identity, so a forged id can't grant a higher limit, and an unsigned/invalid key is rejected before the limiter runs.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "We figure out who you are from your verified key, not from what you claim in the request, so you can't ask for someone else's limit."
**💬 One-liner:** "Identity comes from the verified token, limit from the server — nothing client-supplied is trusted."

**❓ "How do you protect the admin/config API itself?"**
**The mechanism:** role-based access control, every change audited with actor + version, and validation (no zero/negative rates). Changing a limit is treated as a sensitive, abuse-relevant action.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Only authorized operators can change limits, and every change is logged with who did it."
**💬 One-liner:** "RBAC plus an audit log on every rule change — limit changes are sensitive actions."

**❓ "What's the most expensive part of this system, and how would you cut cost?"**
**The mechanism:** the Redis node count dominates because we size for ops/s, not bytes (~40 nodes with replicas). The biggest lever is local token leasing and key-splitting, which cut central ops so we can run fewer shards; if traffic is well under ceiling, collapse to one primary+replica.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "It's the number of fast in-memory machines, and we shrink that by doing more counting locally so we need fewer of them."
**💬 One-liner:** "Cost is the Redis shard count; leasing cuts central ops so we run fewer shards."

**❓ "Why Redis and not just a database counter or an in-memory map?"**
**The mechanism:** we need a *shared* atomic read-modify-write in <1 ms at 1M/s. A DB row lock is ~5–10 ms and exhausts connection pools at that rate; an in-process map isn't shared, so the limit multiplies by fleet size. Redis is the middle: shared, atomic via Lua, microsecond-fast.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "A database is too slow at this rate and a local map isn't shared — Redis is shared and fast."
**💬 One-liner:** "DB is too slow, local memory isn't shared — Redis is the only one that's both."

**❓ "How would you add a new limiting dimension later — say, limit by IP and by endpoint and by user simultaneously?"**
**The mechanism:** the key is a composite `clientId:scope`; we generalize scope to a list of dimensions and run the check against each relevant bucket, denying if any is exhausted (logical AND of limits). Rules are data, not code, so adding a dimension is a config change plus a key-format extension, no redeploy of the algorithm.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Limits are just keyed buckets, so adding 'per IP' or 'per endpoint' is adding more buckets to check — no rewrite."
**💬 One-liner:** "Limits are data-driven keyed buckets — new dimensions are new keys, checked with an AND."

**❓ "How fresh are your metrics, and could the metrics pipeline ever slow the API?"**
**The mechanism:** never — metrics are aggregated in-memory per node and flushed async through Kafka every ~10 s; the hot path does zero metric writes. Dashboards trail by seconds; if Kafka is down, nodes buffer and drop oldest, but real requests are never blocked.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Metrics ride a separate, slower lane, so they can fall behind but can never slow a real request."
**💬 One-liner:** "Async, aggregated metrics — they can lag but never touch the hot path."

**❓ "How do you prevent the global guard or any single counter from becoming a bottleneck of its own?"**
**The mechanism:** the global guard is itself sharded/approximate (e.g. sampled or leased), not a single hot key. Any true single coordinator is avoided; where a global view is needed it's eventually-consistent, not a synchronous chokepoint.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "We never funnel everything through one counter — even the global guard is spread out and approximate."
**💬 One-liner:** "No single global counter on the hot path — the global guard is sharded and approximate."

> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "A distributed rate limiter is a bouncer in front of every API call that answers one question — is this client over its limit — fast enough to be invisible. The design splits into two halves. The read-and-decide half lives in the API gateway and a sharded, in-memory Redis cluster: each client has a token bucket, and a Lua script does refill-check-decrement as one atomic step, so the check is about a millisecond and two servers can never both spend the last token. The hard half is staying correct *and* fast at a million requests a second: the binding problems are the hot key — one giant client overrunning a single shard — which I solve with local token leasing and key-splitting, and the failure case, where the limiter must degrade to a local approximate limit rather than ever take the API down. The two halves connect through a control plane that pushes limit changes fleet-wide over pub/sub in seconds, and an async metrics pipeline through Kafka that observes everything without touching the hot path. Every hard decision traces back to one principle: buy accuracy only where it's cheap, and never let correctness cost you availability or the latency budget."
